summary: Jeff Atwood introduces the Code Snippets feature in Visual Studio .NET 2005, explaining the three types available: simple expansion, template expansion with variables, and surround snippets. He walks through the keyboard shortcuts needed to invoke each type and notes that VB.NET's implementation is more robust than C#'s, with many more built-in snippets and automatic Import statement handling. The post also covers snippet management options and third-party tools like Snippy for creating custom snippets without editing raw XML.
key_points: Code Snippets in VS.NET 2005 come in three flavors: simple expansion, template expansion with variables, and surround snippets
VB.NET ships with significantly more snippets than C# and supports automatic Import statement insertion
Snippets are stored as XML files with a .snippet extension in language-specific folders
While VS.NET provides snippet management UI, there's no built-in editor for creating new snippets
Third-party tools like Snippy provide a GUI alternative to editing raw XML snippet files
money_quotes: One of the most enjoyable new features in Visual Studio .NET 2005 is Code Snippets.
VB.NET's implementation of snippets is a bit more robust than the one in C#.
VB.NET has a zillion snippets shipping in the box where C# has maybe... two dozen?
Assuming you don't enjoy editing raw XML files, it might be easier to go with Microsoft's Snippy.
key_insight: Code Snippets in VS.NET 2005 offer a significant productivity boost through keyboard-driven code expansion, though the implementation quality varies between languages.
